questions.q: What is the primary function of nucleic acids in living organisms?
questions.explanation: Nucleic acids store and transmit genetic information from one generation to the next. They are essential for the synthesis of proteins and regulation of metabolism.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a monosaccharide?
questions.explanation: Monosaccharides are simple sugars that cannot be hydrolyzed further to produce simpler sugars. Glucose is an example of a monosaccharide.

questions.q: What is the primary function of lipids in living organisms?
questions.explanation: Lipids are an essential source of energy for living organisms. They are stored in the form of triglycerides in adipose tissue.

questions.q: Which of the following amino acids is essential for humans?
questions.explanation: Humans cannot synthesize histidine from other amino acids and must obtain it through their diet. Therefore, histidine is an essential amino acid for humans.

questions.q: What is the process by which cells obtain energy from glucose?
questions.explanation: Respiration is the process by which cells obtain energy from glucose in the form of ATP. This process involves the breakdown of glucose to produce energy.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a disaccharide?
questions.explanation: Disaccharides are composed of two monosaccharides joined together. Sucrose, also known as table sugar, is an example of a disaccharide.

questions.q: What is the role of ATP in cellular metabolism?
questions.explanation: ATP is the primary energy currency of the cell. It stores energy in the form of phosphate bonds and transfers this energy to other molecules during cellular metabolism.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a polysaccharide?
questions.explanation: Polysaccharides are composed of many monosaccharides joined together. Starch is an example of a polysaccharide and serves as a storage form of glucose in plants.

questions.q: What is the primary function of enzymes in living organisms?
questions.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions in living organisms. They play a crucial role in regulating metabolism and maintaining homeostasis.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of an isozyme?
questions.explanation: Isozymes are different forms of an enzyme that catalyze the same reaction but have different properties or functions. Lactate dehydrogenase is an example of an isozyme.

questions.q: What is the role of coenzymes in enzyme-catalyzed reactions?
questions.explanation: Coenzymes are non-protein molecules that bind to enzymes and facilitate the transfer of energy or functional groups during enzyme-catalyzed reactions.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a cofactor?
questions.explanation: Cofactors are non-protein molecules that bind to enzymes and facilitate their activity. NAD+ is an example of a cofactor.

questions.q: What is the process by which enzymes are activated or inactivated?
questions.explanation: Phosphorylation is the process by which enzymes are activated or inactivated through the addition or removal of a phosphate group.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a lipoprotein?
questions.explanation: Lipoproteins are molecules that consist of lipids and proteins. Chylomicrons are an example of a lipoprotein and serve as a transport vehicle for dietary lipids.

questions.q: What is the role of lipolysis in lipid metabolism?
questions.explanation: Lipolysis is the process by which triglycerides are broken down into fatty acids and glycerol. This process releases energy from lipids and makes it available for other cellular processes.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a phospholipid?
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are a class of lipids that contain a phosphate group. Phosphatidylcholine is an example of a phospholipid and serves as a component of cell membranes.

questions.q: What is the role of the citric acid cycle in cellular metabolism?
questions.explanation: The citric acid cycle is a series of chemical reactions that occur in the mitochondria and produce energy for the cell. It is also known as the TCA cycle.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a heme protein?
questions.explanation: Heme proteins are a class of proteins that contain a heme group. Hemoglobin is an example of a heme protein and serves as a transport molecule for oxygen in the blood.

questions.q: What is the role of the electron transport chain in cellular metabolism?
questions.explanation: The electron transport chain is a series of protein complexes that occur in the mitochondria and produce ATP during cellular respiration.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a non-protein amino acid?
questions.explanation: Non-protein amino acids are amino acids that are not incorporated into proteins but serve other functions in the cell. Gamma-aminobutyric acid is an example of a non-protein amino acid.
